title: Tutorial: Reviewing an Enzyme Experiment's Results

turns.speaker: Dr Halvorsen
turns.text: Come in, Amira. So, you wanted to go through the results of your catalase experiment before you write it up. You were measuring how temperature affects the rate of the reaction, is that right?

turns.speaker: Amira
turns.text: Yes. I measured how quickly oxygen was produced at five different temperatures, from ten degrees up to fifty. But some of my results look strange, and I'm not sure why.

turns.speaker: Dr Halvorsen
turns.text: Let's look at them together. Overall the shape is what we'd expect — the rate rose as it got warmer, peaked, and then dropped. At what temperature was the reaction fastest?

turns.speaker: Amira
turns.text: At forty degrees. After that, at fifty, it fell sharply, which I think is the enzyme being denatured — the heat changing its shape so it stops working.

turns.speaker: Dr Halvorsen
turns.text: That's exactly right, and it's the key finding — an optimum at forty, then denaturing above it. Good. Now, the strange points. Let's take them one at a time. Your ten-degree reading — you have it much higher than the twenty-degree one, which makes no sense. Any idea why?

turns.speaker: Amira
turns.text: I think that was the very first trial I did, before I'd got the technique right. I was probably slow starting the timer, so it looked faster than it really was.

turns.speaker: Dr Halvorsen
turns.text: Yes — that's simple human reaction-time error at the start. The fix is to repeat that trial now you're practised. Next, your thirty-degree result is all over the place — the three repeats don't agree at all. What happened there?

turns.speaker: Amira
turns.text: Ah — that's the batch of liver I used for those. It had been sitting out and I think it had partly dried out, so the amount of enzyme wasn't consistent between the repeats.

turns.speaker: Dr Halvorsen
turns.text: A contamination-slash-consistency problem with the material itself — well spotted. Always use fresh material from the same source. And the fifty-degree point — you've only got one reading there, not three.

turns.speaker: Amira
turns.text: That one I'll admit — I simply ran out of time and couldn't do the repeats. So it's just missing data, nothing clever.

turns.speaker: Dr Halvorsen
turns.text: Honest, and that's fine — note it as a limitation. Now, an important methodological point. To compare temperatures fairly, what had to stay the same across all your trials?

turns.speaker: Amira
turns.text: The things I kept constant were the concentration of the hydrogen peroxide, the volume of it, and the mass of the liver. Only the temperature changed.

turns.speaker: Dr Halvorsen
turns.text: Good — those are your control variables, and controlling them is what makes it a fair test. So, for the write-up, how should you handle that odd ten-degree point?

turns.speaker: Dr Halvorsen
turns.text: No — never quietly delete data. Keep it, mark it clearly as an anomaly, and explain why you think it's unreliable. Then draw your line of best fit through the trustworthy points. Deleting data without saying so is, frankly, close to dishonesty.

turns.speaker: Amira
turns.text: Understood — flag it, don't hide it. And for the conclusion?

turns.speaker: Dr Halvorsen
turns.text: State the optimum temperature clearly, explain the denaturing, and be honest about the limitations — the missing repeats and the dried-out sample. A good scientist reports the mess, not just the tidy bits. Come back if you get stuck on the graph.

turns.speaker: Amira
turns.text: I will. Thank you so much — I feel far more confident about writing it up now.

questionGroups.prompt: Choose the correct letter, A, B or C.
questionGroups.instructions_html: At which temperature was the reaction fastest?

questionGroups.prompt: Choose the correct letter, A, B or C.
questionGroups.instructions_html: Why did the rate fall sharply at 50 degrees?

options.text: the enzyme was denatured by the heat
there was not enough hydrogen peroxide

questions.prompt_text: At 50 degrees the rate dropped because

questionGroups.prompt: What was the likely cause of the problem at each temperature?
questionGroups.instructions_html: Choose the correct cause A–E for each result and select the correct letter.

options.text: slow reaction time starting the timer
the liver had dried out and was inconsistent
the repeats were not done (missing data)

questionGroups.prompt: Choose the correct letter, A, B or C.
questionGroups.instructions_html: What does the tutor say Amira should do with the anomalous 10-degree point?

options.text: keep it but mark it as an anomaly and explain it
use it as the main result

questions.prompt_text: The variables kept the same in every trial are called the ______ variables.
The heat stops the enzyme working by causing it to become ______.
